---
title: "Data Cleaning & Preprocessing"
author: "GROUP IV"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: true
  
---

## Overview

This document performs all data cleaning and preprocessing steps on the
**Lightcast 2024** job postings dataset. After running all cells, the
cleaned `df` object is ready to use — copy this file and continue writing
your own analysis directly below Section 5.

### Dataset Context

The Lightcast 2024 dataset is pre-filtered to **data, analytics, and tech
occupations** (~72,000 postings, 131 columns). Key quirks to be aware of:

| Issue | Detail |
|---|---|
| `TITLE` | Hash/ID string — **not** human-readable. Use `TITLE_RAW` for job titles. |
| `COMPANY` | Numeric ID — **not** human-readable. Use `COMPANY_NAME` or `COMPANY_RAW`. |
| `SALARY` / `SALARY_FROM` / `SALARY_TO` | ~55–58 % missing by design (employers often omit salary). **Do not drop** these columns. |
| `MAX_EDULEVELS_NAME` | 77 % missing — use `MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME` instead. |
| `LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME` | 75 % missing — use `NAICS_2022_6_NAME` for industry. |

---

## 0. Install & Load Packages

```{python}
import subprocess, sys

required = ["gdown", "pandas", "numpy", "missingno", "matplotlib", "seaborn"]
for pkg in required:
    subprocess.check_call([sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", "-q", pkg])

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import missingno as msno

pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 60)
pd.set_option("display.float_format", "{:,.2f}".format)
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")

print("All packages loaded.")
```

---

## 1. Download & Load Raw Data

```{python}
import gdown, os

FILE_ID = "1V2GCHGt2dkFGqVBeoUFckU4IhUgk4ocQ"
RAW_PATH = "lightcast_2024.csv"

if not os.path.exists(RAW_PATH):
    gdown.download(
        f"https://drive.google.com/uc?id={FILE_ID}",
        RAW_PATH,
        quiet=False
    )
else:
    print(f"File already exists: {RAW_PATH}")

df_raw = pd.read_csv(RAW_PATH, low_memory=False)
print(f"Raw shape: {df_raw.shape[0]:,} rows × {df_raw.shape[1]} columns")
```

---

## 2. Raw Data Inspection

### 2.1 Column List & Dtypes

```{python}
print(f"Total columns: {df_raw.shape[1]}")
print(f"\nDtype summary:")
print(df_raw.dtypes.value_counts().to_string())
print(f"\nAll columns:")
for c in df_raw.columns:
    print(f"  {c:<50s}  [{df_raw[c].dtype}]")
```

### 2.2 Missing Value Profile

```{python}
# NOTE: Several columns are intentionally high-missing:
#   SALARY / SALARY_FROM / SALARY_TO  ~55-58%  (employers often omit salary)
#   LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME            ~75%     (sector taxonomy coverage gap)
#   MAX_EDULEVELS_NAME                ~77%     (max education rarely specified)
# These are KEPT and handled separately — they are not data quality errors.

missing = pd.DataFrame({
    "missing_count": df_raw.isnull().sum(),
    "missing_pct":   df_raw.isnull().mean() * 100
}).sort_values("missing_pct", ascending=False)

print("Columns with missing values:")
print(missing[missing["missing_count"] > 0].to_string())
```

### 2.3 Missing Value Heatmap

```{python}
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
msno.heatmap(df_raw, cmap="coolwarm")
plt.title("Missing Values Correlation Heatmap — Raw Data", fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### 2.4 Basic Statistics

```{python}
print("=== Numeric Columns ===")
display(df_raw.describe(include=[np.number]).T)

print("\n=== Object Columns (sample top value) ===")
for c in df_raw.select_dtypes("object").columns:
    n   = df_raw[c].nunique()
    top = df_raw[c].value_counts().index[0] if n > 0 else "N/A"
    print(f"  {c:<45s}  unique={n:6,}  top='{str(top)[:50]}'")
```

---

## 3. Data Cleaning

```{python}
df = df_raw.copy()
print(f"Starting shape: {df.shape}")
```

### 3.1 Drop Tracking & Redundant Columns

We remove metadata columns (hash IDs, URLs, internal tracking) and older
NAICS / SOC hierarchy levels that are superseded by the 2022 / 2021
versions we keep.

**Columns retained:**

- `NAICS_2022_2` through `NAICS_2022_6` (and their `_NAME` variants) —
  current industry classification
- `SOC_2021_2` through `SOC_2021_5` (and `_NAME` variants) — current
  occupation classification
- All `LOT_*` occupation taxonomy columns

```{python}
columns_to_drop = [
    # Internal metadata / tracking
    "ID", "URL", "ACTIVE_URLS", "ACTIVE_SOURCES_INFO",
    "DUPLICATES", "LAST_UPDATED_TIMESTAMP", "LAST_UPDATED_DATE",
    # Older shorthand NAICS codes (superseded by NAICS_2022_* series)
    "NAICS2", "NAICS3", "NAICS4", "NAICS5", "NAICS6",
    # Older SOC codes without year suffix (superseded by SOC_2021_* series)
    "SOC_2", "SOC_2_NAME", "SOC_3", "SOC_3_NAME", "SOC_5", "SOC_5_NAME",
]

existing_drops = [c for c in columns_to_drop if c in df.columns]
df.drop(columns=existing_drops, inplace=True)

print(f"Dropped {len(existing_drops)} columns: {existing_drops}")
print(f"Shape after step 3.1: {df.shape}")
```

### 3.2 Drop High-Missing Columns (with Salary Protection)

We drop columns where **more than 50 % of values are missing**, but
**explicitly protect** salary and education columns from this rule.

**Why protect salary columns?**
`SALARY`, `SALARY_FROM`, and `SALARY_TO` are each ~55–58 % missing because
many employers choose not to post salary information — this is normal for
job postings data, not a data quality issue. The remaining ~42 % (≈ 30,000
rows) provides a sufficient sample for salary analysis. Dropping them would
silently break all downstream salary work.

```{python}
# Columns to protect from the >50% missing drop rule
PROTECT_COLS = [
    "SALARY", "SALARY_FROM", "SALARY_TO", "ORIGINAL_PAY_PERIOD",
    "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE", "MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
    "LIGHTCAST_SECTORS", "LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME",
    "MIN_EDULEVELS", "MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME",
    "MAX_EDULEVELS", "MAX_EDULEVELS_NAME",
]
protect_existing = [c for c in PROTECT_COLS if c in df.columns]
non_protected    = [c for c in df.columns if c not in protect_existing]

# Apply 50% threshold only to non-protected columns
keep_non_prot = [c for c in non_protected if df[c].isnull().mean() < 0.5]
cols_to_keep  = keep_non_prot + protect_existing
dropped_count = df.shape[1] - len(cols_to_keep)
df = df[cols_to_keep]

print(f"Protected columns: {protect_existing}")
print(f"Dropped {dropped_count} high-missing non-protected columns.")
print(f"Shape after step 3.2: {df.shape}")
```

### 3.3 Remove Duplicate Postings

We deduplicate on the combination of job title + company + state + posting
date. This removes re-posted or syndicated listings that represent the same
position.

```{python}
dup_subset = [
    c for c in ["TITLE_RAW", "COMPANY_NAME", "STATE_NAME", "POSTED"]
    if c in df.columns
]
print(f"Deduplication key: {dup_subset}")

before = len(df)
df.drop_duplicates(subset=dup_subset, keep="first", inplace=True)
removed = before - len(df)

print(f"Removed {removed:,} duplicate rows ({removed/before*100:.1f}% of pre-dedup total).")
print(f"Shape after step 3.3: {df.shape}")
```

### 3.4 Build Unified Salary Column (`SALARY_MID`)

We create a single salary column by taking the midpoint of `SALARY_FROM`
and `SALARY_TO`. Where the midpoint is unavailable we fall back to the
`SALARY` column.

> **Important:** Missing salary values are left as `NaN` — we do **not**
> impute them with the median or mean. All downstream salary analyses
> should use `.dropna(subset=["SALARY_MID"])` or filter to
> `df[df["SALARY_MID"].notna()]` to work only with real data, preserving
> the true salary distribution.

```{python}
if "SALARY_FROM" in df.columns and "SALARY_TO" in df.columns:
    df["SALARY_MID"] = df[["SALARY_FROM", "SALARY_TO"]].mean(axis=1)
    if "SALARY" in df.columns:
        df["SALARY_MID"] = df["SALARY_MID"].fillna(df["SALARY"])
    print("Created SALARY_MID = (SALARY_FROM + SALARY_TO) / 2  [fallback: SALARY]")
elif "SALARY" in df.columns:
    df["SALARY_MID"] = df["SALARY"]
    print("Created SALARY_MID from SALARY column.")
else:
    print("WARNING: No salary source columns found. Check step 3.2 protect list.")

if "SALARY_MID" in df.columns:
    n_sal     = df["SALARY_MID"].notna().sum()
    sal_clean = df[
        (df["SALARY_MID"] > 10_000) & (df["SALARY_MID"] < 500_000)
    ]["SALARY_MID"]
    print(f"\nSalary coverage:  {n_sal:,} postings with data ({n_sal/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")
    print(f"Plausible range:  ${sal_clean.min():,.0f} – ${sal_clean.max():,.0f}")
    print(f"Median:           ${sal_clean.median():,.0f}")
    print(f"Mean:             ${sal_clean.mean():,.0f}")
```

### 3.5 Fill Categorical Missing Values

Remaining `NaN` values in key categorical columns are replaced with the
string `"Unknown"` so they appear explicitly in charts and model features
rather than being silently excluded.

```{python}
fill_unknown = [
    "NAICS_2022_6_NAME",    # 6-digit industry (primary industry column)
    "NAICS_2022_2_NAME",    # 2-digit broad industry
    "SOC_2021_4_NAME",      # SOC occupation name
    "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME",     # Remote / Hybrid / On-site
    "STATE_NAME",           # US state
    "MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME",   # Minimum education required
    "LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME",    # Lightcast occupation category (6 groups)
    "LOT_OCCUPATION_NAME",       # Lightcast occupation (broader)
    "EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME", # Full-time / Part-time / Contract
    "LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME",    # Lightcast sector (75% missing — use with caution)
]

filled = []
for col in fill_unknown:
    if col in df.columns:
        n_filled = df[col].isna().sum()
        df[col].fillna("Unknown", inplace=True)
        filled.append(f"  {col:<45s}  ({n_filled:,} values filled)")

print("Filled 'Unknown' in:")
print("\n".join(filled))
```

---

## 4. Column Reference Guide

A quick-reference map of the most useful columns for downstream analysis.

```{python}
def find_col(df, candidates):
    """Return first matching column name (exact, then fuzzy)."""
    for c in candidates:
        if c in df.columns:
            return c
    for cand in candidates:
        for col in df.columns:
            if cand.upper() in col.upper():
                return col
    return None

# ── Key column aliases ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
COL_TITLE      = find_col(df, ["TITLE_RAW", "TITLE_NAME"])
# ↑ Use TITLE_RAW for human-readable job titles.
#   TITLE is a hash ID and should NOT be used for display or analysis.

COL_OCCUPATION = find_col(df, ["LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME", "LOT_OCCUPATION_NAME",
                                "SOC_2021_4_NAME"])
COL_INDUSTRY   = find_col(df, ["NAICS_2022_6_NAME", "NAICS_2022_4_NAME",
                                "NAICS_2022_2_NAME"])
COL_IND_BROAD  = find_col(df, ["NAICS_2022_2_NAME"])
COL_STATE      = find_col(df, ["STATE_NAME", "STATE"])
COL_REMOTE     = find_col(df, ["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME", "REMOTE_TYPE"])
COL_SALARY     = find_col(df, ["SALARY_MID", "SALARY", "SALARY_FROM"])
COL_COMPANY    = find_col(df, ["COMPANY_NAME", "COMPANY_RAW"])
# ↑ Use COMPANY_NAME/COMPANY_RAW for readable company names.
#   COMPANY is a numeric ID and is NOT human-readable.

COL_SKILLS     = find_col(df, ["SKILLS_NAME", "SKILL_NAME", "SKILLS"])
COL_SOC        = find_col(df, ["SOC_2021_4_NAME", "SOC_2021_5_NAME"])
COL_POSTED     = find_col(df, ["POSTED", "POSTING_DATE"])
COL_EDU        = find_col(df, ["MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME", "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME"])
# ↑ Prefer MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME (near-complete).
#   MAX_EDULEVELS_NAME is 77% missing and should be used with caution.

COL_EXP        = find_col(df, ["MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE", "MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"])
COL_EMPTYPE    = find_col(df, ["EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME"])

# ── Print resolved mapping ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
print(f"{'Variable':<18s}  {'Resolved Column':<45s}  Coverage")
print("-" * 85)
for name, col in [
    ("COL_TITLE",      COL_TITLE),
    ("COL_OCCUPATION", COL_OCCUPATION),
    ("COL_INDUSTRY",   COL_INDUSTRY),
    ("COL_IND_BROAD",  COL_IND_BROAD),
    ("COL_STATE",      COL_STATE),
    ("COL_REMOTE",     COL_REMOTE),
    ("COL_SALARY",     COL_SALARY),
    ("COL_COMPANY",    COL_COMPANY),
    ("COL_SKILLS",     COL_SKILLS),
    ("COL_SOC",        COL_SOC),
    ("COL_POSTED",     COL_POSTED),
    ("COL_EDU",        COL_EDU),
    ("COL_EXP",        COL_EXP),
    ("COL_EMPTYPE",    COL_EMPTYPE),
]:
    if col and col in df.columns:
        n    = df[col].notna().sum()
        pct  = n / len(df) * 100
        cov  = f"{n:,}  ({pct:.1f}%)"
        status = ""
    else:
        cov    = "N/A"
        status = "  ← NOT FOUND"
    print(f"  {name:<16s}  {str(col):<45s}  {cov}{status}")
```

---

## 5. Cleaned Dataset Summary

```{python}
print("=" * 60)
print("  CLEANED DATASET SUMMARY")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"  Rows:     {df.shape[0]:,}")
print(f"  Columns:  {df.shape[1]}")

print(f"\n  Missing values remaining:")
remaining_miss = df.isnull().sum()
remaining_miss = remaining_miss[remaining_miss > 0].sort_values(ascending=False)
for col, cnt in remaining_miss.items():
    print(f"    {col:<45s}  {cnt:,}  ({cnt/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")

if COL_SALARY and COL_SALARY in df.columns:
    sal = df[(df[COL_SALARY] > 10_000) & (df[COL_SALARY] < 500_000)][COL_SALARY]
    print(f"\n  Salary (SALARY_MID, valid range $10k–$500k):")
    print(f"    Count:   {len(sal):,}")
    print(f"    Median:  ${sal.median():,.0f}")
    print(f"    Mean:    ${sal.mean():,.0f}")
    print(f"    Std:     ${sal.std():,.0f}")
    print(f"    10th:    ${sal.quantile(0.1):,.0f}")
    print(f"    90th:    ${sal.quantile(0.9):,.0f}")
```

---

## Appendix: Full Column List (Cleaned)

```{python}
print(f"All {df.shape[1]} columns in cleaned dataset:\n")
for i, c in enumerate(df.columns, 1):
    n   = df[c].notna().sum()
    pct = n / len(df) * 100
    print(f"  {i:3d}.  {c:<50s}  [{df[c].dtype}]  {pct:.0f}% non-null")
```
